hook: The world's oldest Egyptian museum, where Champollion cracked the hieroglyphs.
facts: Founded in 1824 when King Charles Felix bought the Drovetti collection of over 5,000 pieces.
Holds more than 40,000 artefacts, second only to Cairo in importance.
Jean-Francois Champollion studied the Turin papyri while deciphering hieroglyphic writing in the 1820s.
Includes the rock-cut Ellesiya chapel, gifted by Egypt for Italy's role in saving the Nubian temples.
w: Cairo aside, no city holds more of ancient Egypt than this one, and it got there first: founded 1824 on a French consul's 5,000-piece haul. Champollion came to test his hieroglyph breakthroughs on the Turin papyri. Forty thousand objects, including a whole Nubian chapel rescued stone by stone. Egypt by the Po.

hook: A railway-tunnel monument that folklore reads as the gate to the underworld.
facts: Erected in 1879 to mark the Frejus railway tunnel between Italy and France.
Titans pile up around the base, crowned by a winged genius meant to symbolise reason.
The square lies west of the centre, where Roman tradition placed the realm of the dead.
It was once a site of public executions, feeding its grim reputation.
legend: Legend names Piazza Statuto a vertex of the black-magic triangle with London and San Francisco.
They say the winged figure atop the fountain is really Lucifer, and a sealed hatch nearby marks the gates of hell.
w: Officially it salutes the Frejus rail tunnel, all heaped Titans and a winged genius of reason. Turinese folklore reads it darker: out west, where the Romans put their dead, on a former gallows-ground besides. Legend says the winged figure is Lucifer and a sealed hatch nearby opens onto hell. Black-magic corner.

hook: A four-seasons fountain locals decode as a Masonic map to hidden knowledge.
facts: Sculpted by Giovanni Riva and unveiled in 1929 around the theme of the four seasons.
Two male figures are read as Boaz and Jachin, the pillars guarding the path to knowledge.
A rectangular gap between them is interpreted as a threshold for the initiated.
It was moved here from a site near the cathedral over unease about its symbolism.
legend: Esoteric tradition calls it a Masonic cipher and a marker of Turin's white-magic heart, its rear figures aligned to a hidden gateway.
w: Front-on, just spring, summer, autumn, winter. Look closer and the esoteric reading takes over: the two men become Boaz and Jachin, temple pillars flanking a rectangular gap said to be a threshold for initiates. Legend ties it to Turin's white magic. They moved it here once the Masonic symbols rattled the church.

hook: Down into the 1706 siege tunnels where one sapper blew himself up to save the city.
facts: The museum sits over surviving counter-mine galleries of the old citadel, part of a 14 km network.
On the night of 29-30 August 1706, miner Pietro Micca lit a powder charge to stop French grenadiers, dying in the blast.
His sacrifice helped lift the French siege of Turin during the War of the Spanish Succession.
The civic museum opened in 1961 with maps, models and 18th-century weapons.
w: In 1706 the French were tunnelling under the citadel to crack Turin open. A miner from Biella named Pietro Micca lit a powder charge on a staircase too short to outrun, and blew the breach shut at the cost of his life. The museum drops you into those damp counter-mine galleries. Fourteen kilometres of war underground.

hook: The discredited skulls and brains of a man who thought crime was written on the face.
facts: Cesare Lombroso (1835-1909) assembled it to prove a biological 'born criminal', a theory now wholly discredited.
Holds skulls, skeletons, brains, death masks and prisoners' objects from around the world.
Reopened in critically reframed form in 2009 for the centenary of Lombroso's death.
Shares the Palazzo degli Istituti Anatomici with the fruit and human-anatomy museums.
w: Lombroso believed criminals were born, their guilt legible in a jaw or a brow, and he collected skulls, brains and death masks to prove it. The science collapsed; the cabinet survived. Reopened in 2009 with panels that pick the pseudo-science apart rather than parade it. A museum of a brilliant, dangerous mistake.

hook: Eleven hundred uncannily lifelike fake fruits, blemishes and all.
facts: Displays around 1,100 artificial fruits modelled mainly by Francesco Garnier Valletti (1808-89).
Valletti devised a secret casting paste of alabaster, wax, honey and tree resin.
Over 600 apple and pear varieties are recorded, many now extinct.
Opened in 2007 inside the Palazzo degli Istituti Anatomici.
w: A confectioner turned obsessive, Garnier Valletti spent decades casting fruit from a secret paste of wax, honey and resin, every bruise and blemish faithful. The result: 1,100 fakes so true they preserve 600 apple and pear varieties now lost to the world. A pomological archive you could mistake for a greengrocer's.

hook: A 15th-century village that never existed, built whole for an 1884 fair.
facts: Raised for the 1884 Italian General Exhibition as its Ancient Art section.
Architect Alfredo D'Andrade copied details from over 40 real castles and churches across Piedmont and the Aosta Valley.
Too popular to demolish, it was kept and folded into the city's civic museums.
The Rocca and Borgo closed for restoration in 2024, reopening from 2026.
w: Built in 1884 for a fair and meant to be torn down after, this 'medieval' hamlet on the Po proved too charming to bin. D'Andrade stitched it from details cribbed off 40 genuine Piedmontese castles, so every gate and fresco quotes something real in a fake town. A forgery assembled with scholarly care. Restoration runs to 2026.

hook: A nine-floor house just 54 centimetres wide at its thin end.
facts: Officially Casa Scaccabarozzi, built by Mole architect Alessandro Antonelli around 1840.
The trapezoidal plan narrows to 54 cm on one side and widens to 4.35 m on the other.
Stands 24 metres over nine levels, seven above ground and two below.
Its ochre colour and slim wedge shape earned the nickname 'slice of polenta'.
w: Handed an absurd wedge of leftover land, Antonelli, who would later raise the Mole, took it as a dare and built nine floors on it. One end runs 54 centimetres wide, the colour a ripe maize yellow, hence the name. He lived inside his own joke to prove it would stand. A house thin as a slice of polenta.

hook: The 1763 cafe that claims the layered coffee-and-chocolate drink as its own.
facts: Opened on Piazza della Consolata in 1763 and long run entirely by women.
The bicerin layers espresso, drinking chocolate and frothed milk in a small glass.
Cavour reportedly waited here for the royal family to finish their devotions at the Consolata.
Named Italy's best bar by Gambero Rosso in 2001.
w: Open since 1763 and for generations run only by women, this marble box opposite the Consolata gave the city its bicerin: espresso, hot chocolate and milk layered in a little glass, never stirred. Cavour took his here while dodging Mass next door. Order it warm, sit under the old clock. A bit of everything, by design.

hook: A jewel-box cafe where the Italian tramezzino sandwich was born.
facts: On Piazza Castello since 1907, in a tiny gilded Art Nouveau room.
A plaque credits Angela Demichelis Nebiolo with inventing the tramezzino here in 1926.
The owners had emigrated to Detroit, then returned and bought the cafe in 1925.
Poet D'Annunzio coined the word tramezzino to replace the English 'sandwich'.
w: Barely bigger than a railway carriage, all gilt and tooled leather, this 1907 counter on Piazza Castello gave Italy the tramezzino. A returning emigre couple, fresh from Detroit, served soft crustless triangles in 1926; D'Annunzio supplied the name to spite the word 'sandwich'. Stand at the bar where it started.

hook: A film museum spiralling up inside Europe's once-tallest brick tower.
facts: Founded in 1941 by collector Maria Adriana Prolo, who spent decades amassing the hoard.
Housed in the Mole Antonelliana, begun in 1863 and rising to 167.5 metres.
Exhibits climb from shadow theatre and magic lanterns to special effects and VR.
A glass lift, first run in 1961, shoots up the open central void to an 85-metre terrace.
w: One woman, Maria Adriana Prolo, spent a lifetime hoarding film history, and it ended up inside the Mole, the brick spire that nearly bankrupted the city to build. The collection spirals up the hollow shaft from magic lanterns to VR, a glass lift threading the void to the terrace. Cinema stacked inside a folly.

hook: Twelve kilometres of sculpted arcades where Primo Levi rests.
facts: Turin's largest cemetery, with over 400,000 burials.
Some 12 km of arcaded porticoes carry Liberty, Art Deco and Novecento funerary sculpture.
Works survive by sculptors including Bistolfi, Calandra and Canonica.
Among those buried here are writer Edmondo De Amicis and Auschwitz survivor Primo Levi.
w: An open-air gallery of grief: twelve kilometres of arcades crowded with Liberty angels and Deco mourners, the chisel-work of Bistolfi and Canonica. Over 400,000 lie here, among them De Amicis and Primo Levi, whose plain Jewish-section stone carries his Auschwitz number. Come for the sculpture, stay for the quiet.

hook: The shroud's whole story, told in the crypt beneath its own church.
facts: Set in the crypt of the Santissimo Sudario church, founded by the Confraternity in 1936.
The actual shroud stays in the cathedral and is shown only by papal decree.
A full-size copy and decades of scientific imaging stand in for the relic.
Centrepiece: the jewelled silver case that held the cloth until the 1997 cathedral fire.
w: The shroud itself stays locked in the cathedral, shown only when a pope says so, so this crypt museum tells its long, contested life instead: photographs, forensic studies, a life-size copy. Its treasure remains the jewelled silver casket that held the cloth until flames nearly took it in 1997. Faith and evidence, side by side.

hook: A Roman gate, a medieval castle and a baroque facade fused into one building.
facts: Two towers preserve the Roman gate where the decumanus of Augusta Taurinorum once began.
The Savoy-Acaja enlarged it into a castle in the early 14th century.
Juvarra added the white baroque front in 1716, left unfinished after 1721.
It has held the City Museum of Ancient Art since 1934.
w: Walk the back and you meet Roman brick: two towers of the gate where Augusta Taurinorum's main street set off. A medieval castle grew over it, then Juvarra slapped a dazzling baroque face across the front in 1716, money ran out, and he never finished. Two thousand years of Turin stacked in one wall. Now an art museum.

hook: A rooftop car test track turned hanging garden above an old Fiat factory.
facts: The Lingotto factory, finished in 1923, sent cars up internal ramps to a rooftop test track.
The 1.5 km oval, banked at each end, let new Fiats run laps in the sky.
Le Corbusier called the plant one of industry's most impressive sights.
In 2021 the track became La Pista 500, a garden of over 40,000 plants.
w: Raw steel went in at the ground floor and finished Fiats spiralled up to be raced on the roof, a banked 1.5 km oval in the sky that floored Le Corbusier. The line closed in 1982; in 2021 the track sprouted 40,000 plants. You can still walk the curve where test drivers once flung cars five floors up.

hook: A gilded 1875 confectioner's tucked inside a glass-roofed arcade.
facts: Two Canavese confectioners opened here in the Galleria Subalpina in 1875.
Interiors layer gilded stucco, carved mahogany, mosaic floors and marble.
Regulars over the years included Einaudi, Pavese and Rita Levi-Montalcini.
A Siena-marble facade carries a bronze allegory of the four seasons.
w: Step out of the rain into the Galleria Subalpina and there it sits, gilded since 1875: mahogany, mirrors, mosaic floor, 44 gas flames' worth of glitter. Two confectioners from the Canavese built it; Einaudi, Pavese and Levi-Montalcini wore down its chairs. Order a gianduiotto and let the chandeliers do the talking.

hook: One of the world's oldest car museums, in the city that built Fiat.
facts: Founded by Carlo Biscaretti di Ruffia, among the oldest automobile museums anywhere.
Over 200 vehicles from some 80 marques fill the riverside galleries.
A 1960 building, restyled in 2011, expanded display space to 19,000 square metres.
Stands on Corso Unita d'Italia near the old Lingotto works.
w: Fitting that one of the planet's oldest car museums sits in Fiat's home town. Over 200 machines from 80 marques run from brass-era curiosities to grand-prix racers, staged like theatre across a riverside hall reworked in 2011. The pioneer steamers alone reward the tram ride south. Turin told as a century of engines.

hook: Sixty Renaissance cartoons by Gaudenzio Ferrari, unique in the world.
facts: The academy's picture gallery opened in 1837 for the teaching of art.
Charles Albert donated 60 large cartoons by Gaudenzio Ferrari and his school in 1832.
Inventories record a plaster-cast collection that swelled to some 850 items.
The gallery holds more than 300 paintings, sculptures and drawings.
w: Behind the fine-art academy hides its strangest treasure: 60 full-size cartoons from Gaudenzio Ferrari's workshop, an entire Renaissance studio's working drawings preserved nowhere else like this. Add ranks of plaster casts and 300-odd paintings, and a teaching collection becomes a quiet marvel. Students still sketch among them.

hook: A carved palace door whose serpent knocker folklore blames on the devil.
facts: Set in Palazzo Trucchi di Levaldigi, built 1673-77 by Amedeo di Castellamonte.
The Paris-commissioned door was carved by Pietro Danesi around 1675.
Its knocker shows two snakes coiling from a demon's mouth.
The palace once housed an early tarot-card workshop, fuel for its black-magic lore.
legend: Legend says the door appeared overnight in a storm after an apprentice summoned the devil, who was sealed forever behind it.
w: Most palace doors get a lion's-head knocker; this one got two serpents writhing out of a demon's mouth, carved in Paris around 1675. Legend says the whole door materialised in a storm after a careless apprentice raised the devil, then trapped him behind it. A tarot workshop once ran inside, which only fed the rumours. Knock if you dare.

hook: Built on the spot of Turin's 1453 'miracle of the host'.
facts: Marks where, on 6 June 1453, a host is said to have risen shining from a fallen monstrance.
The looted sacred bread had come from plundered Exilles in the Val di Susa.
An oratory rose here in 1521; the present church began in 1607 to Vitozzi's design.
Stands on Piazza Corpus Domini beside Via Palazzo di Citta.
legend: Esoteric guides count the miracle site among the points of Turin's white magic.
w: In 1453, the story goes, a thief's mule stumbled here and the stolen host floated up shining like a second sun before sinking into a chalice. The city marked the spot first with an oratory, then this baroque church from 1607. Esoteric guidebooks file it under Turin's white magic. A pavement plaque marks where the mule fell.

hook: Under the royal palace, Leonardo's red-chalk self-portrait waits in the dark.
facts: Founded by Charles Albert in 1837 within the Royal Museums complex.
Holds 13 Leonardo drawings, including the famous sanguine self-portrait of about 1515.
Also keeps Leonardo's Codex on the Flight of Birds.
A purpose-built underground room from 1998 protects the fragile sheets.
w: Charles Albert's book-lined hall hides Italy's most haunting face: Leonardo's red-chalk self-portrait, the prophet-bearded old man, drawn around 1515 and so frail it lives in a climate-sealed vault below. Thirteen of his sheets are here, plus his codex on the flight of birds. The genius, looking back at you from a cellar.

hook: A walled university garden by the Po, laid out in geometric beds since the 1730s.
facts: Rooted in the Orto Regio that Victor Amadeus II established in 1713.
It became part of the university with the founding of a botany chair in 1729.
Beds were laid out in a geometric plan in 1730-31, planted with some 800 species.
It survives beside the Castello del Valentino on the riverbank.
w: Behind a wall in the Valentino, where most stroll past to the river, the university keeps a garden first planted by royal decree in 1713 and laid out in tidy geometric beds by 1731. Eight hundred species went into the original scheme; greenhouses, arboretum and order beds still teach botany. Open seasonally, gloriously overlooked.
